Subject: Re: [spambayes-dev] spoof detector
Body: >> Their physical and structural proximity is not noted. Synthetic >> tokens based on hostname or IP address in the urls will be generated >> if you add x-pick_apart_urls:True to the Tokenizer section of your >> config file. Dave> That doesn't sound like it's doing what I'm asking about. No, it's not, however, you might be surprised how helpful it is to generate tokens for the /8, /16, /24 and /32 address blocks can be. I what I was implying is that maybe you don't need the spoof detection you were asking for if the address tokens generated from the spammer's IP address are spammy. Dave> I want a special token that is generated each time a link's text Dave> is just a URL and the link and the URL text don't point to the Dave> same place. That will require actually parsing the HTML at some level. SpamBayes just sees a stream of tokens. It doesn't really know much (if anything) about compound structure. Dave> Messages with this property are always spam and account for a Dave> large percentage of my unsures. Try these two settings x-pick_apart_urls:True x-lookup_ip:True and see if they help. Dave> From what you say above it looks like pick_apart_urls will Dave> generate tokens describing different parts of a given URL, but Dave> will do nothing to help capture this particular spammy Dave> relationship between enclosed text and actual link. Dave> Or did I misunderstand you? No, I probably misunderstood myself. The IP address hacker is the x-lookup_ip option I believe. They are both helpful though. Skip _______________________________________________ spambayes-dev mailing list bsfegmjrr-hnq@python.org http://mail.python.org/mailman/listinfo/spambayes-dev

Subject: Re: [opensuse] OpenSuse 11
Body: Basil Chupin wrote: > > The need to create a normal, limited access, user in XP Professional > is not something which is enforced in Professional which is why, I > understand, most XP Prof. setups are being run by a home user with > full Admin. rights. I cannot see why the Home edition should be more > security conscious than the Professional edition, and also cost much > less, unless SP1 and SP2 introduced the ability to create > limited-access users in the Home edition. > I believe both versions work the same. The first account has admin rights and many people don't go beyond that to create users with restricted rights. They simply don't realize the difference between admin and user. At least in Linux, at least SUSE, you get warnings about running as root and you're expected to create a user during install. With Ubuntu and some other distros, the first user is an admin user, with rights to create a root password. Additional users are mere mortals. -- Use OpenOffice.org
text:
Re: [opensuse] OpenSuse 11

Basil Chupin wrote: > > The need to create a normal, limited access, user in XP Professional > is not something which is enforced in Professional which is why, I > understand, most XP Prof. setups are being run by a home user with > full Admin. rights. I cannot see why the Home edition should be more > security conscious than the Professional edition, and also cost much > less, unless SP1 and SP2 introduced the ability to create > limited-access users in the Home edition. > I believe both versions work the same. The first account has admin rights and many people don't go beyond that to create users with restricted rights. They simply don't realize the difference between admin and user. At least in Linux, at least SUSE, you get warnings about running as root and you're expected to create a user during install. With Ubuntu and some other distros, the first user is an admin user, with rights to create a root password. Additional users are mere mortals. -- Use OpenOffice.org

Subject: Vi har oppdatert visningsnavnet ditt – dette er grunnen!
Body: Hei! Vi har gjort en liten endring på FINN-profilen din - nærmere bestemt har vi oppdatert visningsnavnet ditt (navnet som er knyttet til FINN-brukeren din, og som står som avsender når du feks. sender meldinger). Hvorfor har vi gjort dette? I det siste har vi sett noen tilfeller der folk med uærlige hensikter har prøvd å gjette de private e-postadressene til FINN-brukere, basert på visningsnavnet deres på FINN. For å gjøre det tryggere for deg og andre, har vi derfor valgt å endre visningsnavnet ditt - slik at det skal bli vanskeligere å gjette seg frem til eposten din. Dette gjelder alle FINN-brukere som har sitt fulle navn i visningsnavnet, eller hvis visningsnavn av en eller annen grunn gjør e-posten deres lett å gjette. Din trygghet er viktig for oss Vi i FINN jobber hver dag for at det skal være enkelt og trygt å kjøpe og selge på vår markedsplass. Vi deler aldri epost-adresseinfoen til våre brukere med andre. Får du likevel en e-post som hevder å komme fra en FINN-bruker, bør du være ekstra oppmerksom – det kan være svindel. Kan man endre det nye visningsnavnet? Selvfølgelig! Du kan når som helst endre visningsnavnet ditt ved å logge inn på Min FINN og Min konto. Vi anbefaler imidlertid at du velger et visningsnavn som ikke gjør det lett å gjette e-postadressen din. Ta gjerne kontakt med oss hvis du har spørsmål – vi er her for å hjelpe! Hilsen oss i FINN

Subject: Re: [patch@31780] CPANPLUS/Internals/Constants.pm for VMS
Body: On 04 Sep 2007, at 07:45, John E. Malmberg wrote: > John E. Malmberg wrote: >> On VMS, if the $Config{make} is either MMK or MMS, then the >> makefile is 'DESCRIP.MMS'. >> Currently on VMS it is believed that $Config{make} is always one >> of those two, however I hope to change that in the future. >> With this change and the others, 20_CPANPLUS-Dist-MM.t passes all >> tests. > > However 06_CPANPLUS-Internals-Constants.t also needs a > corresponding patch as it is checking the name of the build > procedure returned. Thanks, applied -- Jos Boumans How do I prove I'm not crazy to people who are?

Subject: A home-stretch election vibe check
Body: This Week in Politics Experts on Olivia Chow’s lead, and what else we should be talking about ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ THIS WEEK IN POLITICS By David Rider Richard Lautens/Toronto Star With advance voting underway and multiple debates in the rear view mirror, election day on June 26 is coming fast. To do a home-stretch vibe check, I put questions to three independent observers — here’s what they had to say. Do you believe polls showing Olivia Chow with a big lead? It’s significant that repeated polls over time suggest Chow is the frontrunner, Myer Siemiatycki, a Toronto Metropolitan University politics professor emeritus, told me. Others being clustered behind suggests “it has been difficult for the public to parse the differences or distinctiveness of their campaigns.” No rival has yet generated momentum to be an obvious alternative to Chow. Velma Morgan, chair of Operation Black Vote Canada, agreed. “It’s difficult to argue with the polls since they consistently favour (Chow), but we don’t know where the undecided will fall. We are also unaware of data on second choice for declared Chow supporters,” she said. She noted some political polls have turned out to be wrong. There’s no reason to doubt the polls, University of Western Ontario political scientist Zack Taylor said, but “we still have two weeks left, and people may start to tune in more, so movement is possible.” Don't miss a thing from your favourite Star columnists. Always get the latest from the voices that matter most to you: pick the columnists you'd like to follow here, and you’ll get an email every time they have a new article online. How could a non-Chow candidate break through? We’ll hear more “dire warnings” of a Chow mayoralty by rivals declaring themselves the only viable alternative, Siemiatycki predicted. “Doubling down on negativity” might be the only hope for those candidates, he said, but it hasn’t worked so far. Rivals need to draw in other candidates’ support and become the one voters coalesce around, Morgan said. The Chow campaign will try to woo supporters of other progressive candidates to ward off a centre-right candidate coming up the middle. Taylor is surprised no low-polling candidates have, so far, suspended their campaigns because of trouble fundraising, given the overlapping pools of donors. If that happens, it could help consolidate votes for those seen as the most likely to beat Chow. Are you surprised at which issues are getting traction? What deserves more attention? Morgan isn’t surprised safety, housing and transit dominate conversations, but wants to know how closely voters are examining proffered solutions. Mainstream media needs to help them understand candidate pledges, she said, and their potential impacts on disparate communities. Taylor noted issues include housing affordability, community reinvestment and public safety but says none have crystallized as the overriding public concern, although that may happen yet. The campaign has shown that, after years of John Tory’s rule, Torontonians are “open to new directions,” Siemiatycki said. In early campaign days, Ontario Place and the Science Centre dominated headlines, reflecting public unease with provincial plans. Housing, transportation and safety are also hot topics, Siemiatycki said, but he’d like to see candidates talk about countering provincial interventions: “How will they defend Toronto democracy?” Here’s more of the Star’s Toronto election coverage from my colleagues: How wide is Olivia Chow’s lead? Here’s what the latest polls show. Ben Spurr lists six takeaways from this week’s CBC-hosted debate, and I covered this week’s second debate on issues affecting older adults. Advance voting is now open — here’s where and when you can cast your ballot. Listen: Edward Keenan gets an insider look at the race from Jennifer Keesmaat. Olivia Chow’s property tax plan might add up, but she needs to show us the math, Matt Elliott writes. Finally, last week’s edition of This Week in Politics misspelled Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland’s name. David Rider is city hall bureau chief for the Star. A home-stretch election vibe check

This Week in Politics Experts on Olivia Chow’s lead, and what else we should be talking about ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ THIS WEEK IN POLITICS By David Rider Richard Lautens/Toronto Star With advance voting underway and multiple debates in the rear view mirror, election day on June 26 is coming fast. To do a home-stretch vibe check, I put questions to three independent observers — here’s what they had to say. Do you believe polls showing Olivia Chow with a big lead? It’s significant that repeated polls over time suggest Chow is the frontrunner, Myer Siemiatycki, a Toronto Metropolitan University politics professor emeritus, told me. Others being clustered behind suggests “it has been difficult for the public to parse the differences or distinctiveness of their campaigns.” No rival has yet generated momentum to be an obvious alternative to Chow. Velma Morgan, chair of Operation Black Vote Canada, agreed. “It’s difficult to argue with the polls since they consistently favour (Chow), but we don’t know where the undecided will fall. We are also unaware of data on second choice for declared Chow supporters,” she said. She noted some political polls have turned out to be wrong. There’s no reason to doubt the polls, University of Western Ontario political scientist Zack Taylor said, but “we still have two weeks left, and people may start to tune in more, so movement is possible.” Don't miss a thing from your favourite Star columnists. Always get the latest from the voices that matter most to you: pick the columnists you'd like to follow here, and you’ll get an email every time they have a new article online. How could a non-Chow candidate break through? We’ll hear more “dire warnings” of a Chow mayoralty by rivals declaring themselves the only viable alternative, Siemiatycki predicted. “Doubling down on negativity” might be the only hope for those candidates, he said, but it hasn’t worked so far. Rivals need to draw in other candidates’ support and become the one voters coalesce around, Morgan said. The Chow campaign will try to woo supporters of other progressive candidates to ward off a centre-right candidate coming up the middle. Taylor is surprised no low-polling candidates have, so far, suspended their campaigns because of trouble fundraising, given the overlapping pools of donors. If that happens, it could help consolidate votes for those seen as the most likely to beat Chow. Are you surprised at which issues are getting traction? What deserves more attention? Morgan isn’t surprised safety, housing and transit dominate conversations, but wants to know how closely voters are examining proffered solutions. Mainstream media needs to help them understand candidate pledges, she said, and their potential impacts on disparate communities. Taylor noted issues include housing affordability, community reinvestment and public safety but says none have crystallized as the overriding public concern, although that may happen yet. The campaign has shown that, after years of John Tory’s rule, Torontonians are “open to new directions,” Siemiatycki said. In early campaign days, Ontario Place and the Science Centre dominated headlines, reflecting public unease with provincial plans. Housing, transportation and safety are also hot topics, Siemiatycki said, but he’d like to see candidates talk about countering provincial interventions: “How will they defend Toronto democracy?” Here’s more of the Star’s Toronto election coverage from my colleagues: How wide is Olivia Chow’s lead? Here’s what the latest polls show. Ben Spurr lists six takeaways from this week’s CBC-hosted debate, and I covered this week’s second debate on issues affecting older adults. Advance voting is now open — here’s where and when you can cast your ballot. Listen: Edward Keenan gets an insider look at the race from Jennifer Keesmaat. Olivia Chow’s property tax plan might add up, but she needs to show us the math, Matt Elliott writes. Finally, last week’s edition of This Week in Politics misspelled Deputy Prime Minister and Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland’s name. David Rider is city hall bureau chief for the Star.
